message.content:
The bell above the door jingled, a sharp, clear note that cut through the thick, dusty air of the shop. Inside, the world was a symphony of ticks. Hundreds of them, overlapping, clashing, and harmonizing in a chaotic rhythm that seemed to vibrate in the very floorboards.

Leo, no older than seven, stood frozen in the doorway. His sneakers were scuffed, and his hair was a mess of wind-tousled curls. He stared at the walls, which were not walls at all, but shelves upon shelves of clocks. Grandfathers, cuckoos, pocket watches, and strange, skeletal mechanisms made of brass and glass.

"Mind the step, young master," a voice rasped from the shadows.

Leo jumped. Behind a workbench cluttered with magnifying glasses, tweezers, and tiny gears, sat Mr. Thorne. The old man looked as though he had been carved from the same wood as the grandfather clocks. His beard was white and wild, and his eyes, magnified by thick spectacles, blinked slowly.

"I didn't mean to interrupt," Leo said, stepping inside. The door clicked shut behind him, muffling the street noise. "I just... I heard the ticking. It sounded like a heartbeat."

Mr. Thorne smiled, a crack in a dry landscape. "A heartbeat? Or a countdown?" He set down his tweezers and picked up a small, silver pocket watch. He wound it with a gentle twist. *Click-click-click.* "Come closer. But don't touch. These are delicate things. They have tempers."

Leo crept forward, his eyes wide. "Do they all tell the same time?"

Mr. Thorne chuckled, a sound like grinding gears. "Look at the grandfather clock in the corner. And look at the cuck
